sentences.base_language: Donghyeon had been planning the move for months and the day had finally arrived.
sentences.target_language: 동현은 몇 달 동안 이사를 준비해 왔고, 마침내 그날이 찾아왔다.
sentences.romanized: Donghyeon-eun myeot dal dongan isa-reul junbihae watgo, machimnae geunali chajawatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 오다** continuative: 준비해 **왔고** (had been preparing [up to now]). **-아/어 오다** expresses an action or state that began in the past and has continued up to the present: 살아 **왔다** (has been living), 기다려 **왔다** (has been waiting). It emphasises the sustained duration leading up to the current moment, unlike the simple past which just marks completion.
**찾아오다** = to come to (someone/somewhere), to arrive: 그날이 **찾아왔다** (that day came/arrived). **찾아오다** (찾다 look for + 오다 come) implies something sought or awaited has now reached the subject — a vivid way to say a long-anticipated day has finally arrived. Compare: **오다** (simply 'to come'), **찾아오다** (to come as if sought after — more emotionally resonant).

sentences.base_language: His friends came early in the morning to help carry boxes and furniture down to the moving truck.
sentences.target_language: 친구들이 이른 아침에 찾아와서, 박스와 가구를 이삿짐 트럭까지 옮기는 것을 도와주었다.
sentences.romanized: Chingudeul-i ireun achim-e chajawaseo, bakseu-wa gagu-reul isatjim teuleok-kkaji omgineun geot-eul dowajueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어서** sequential: 찾아**와서** 도와주었다 (came over and helped). When **-아/어서** follows a movement verb, the two clauses form a natural sequential pair — first arriving, then helping. The same subject must perform both actions. Compare: **-고** (and — neutral listing of events), **-아/어서** (and then — tighter sequential or causal link).
**-아/어 주다** benefactive: **도와주었다** (helped [as a favour for him]). **-아/어 주다** added to a verb signals that the action is performed for the benefit of another person: 가르쳐 **주다** (teach for someone), 가져다 **주다** (bring for someone). It is one of the most common politeness/consideration markers in Korean conversation.

sentences.base_language: After several trips up and down the stairs, everyone's arms and legs were aching.
sentences.target_language: 계단을 몇 번이나 오르내린 뒤, 모두의 팔과 다리가 쑤셨다.
sentences.romanized: Gyedan-eul myeot beon-ina oreunaerin dwi, modu-eui pal-gwa dari-ga ssusyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-ㄴ/은 뒤** = after doing: 오르내린 **뒤** (after going up and down). **-(으)ㄴ 뒤** and **-(으)ㄴ 후** both mean 'after' and attach to the past modifier form of the verb. They indicate that the second event follows the completion of the first: 먹**은 뒤** 쉬다 (rest after eating). Compare: **-고 나서** (after doing — often emphasises that the first action is fully finished before the next begins).
**몇 번이나** = as many as several times (implying more than expected): **이나** attached to a quantity adds a nuance of surprise or emphasis at the amount: 세 번**이나** (as many as three times), 다섯 개**나** (as many as five). Without **이나**, 몇 번 is a neutral 'several times'; with it, the speaker highlights that the number was notable or taxing.

sentences.base_language: The old apartment looked strangely empty once everything had been removed.
sentences.target_language: 모든 짐을 빼고 나자, 예전 아파트가 묘하게 텅 빈 것처럼 보였다.
sentences.romanized: Modeun jim-eul ppaego naja, yejeon apateu-ga myohage teong bin geotcheoreom boyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 나다** = after finishing doing, once done: 빼**고 나자** (once [everything] had been removed). **-고 나다** emphasises that the first action has been fully completed before the next state occurs: 먹**고 나서** 졸렸다 (felt sleepy after finishing eating). The form **-고 나자** uses **-자** (as soon as/once) to add immediacy — the emptiness became apparent the very moment the last item was taken.
**-(으)ㄴ 것처럼** = as if, as though (resemblance): 텅 빈 **것처럼** 보였다 (looked as if it were completely empty). **것처럼** combines the nominaliser **것** with the comparison particle **처럼** (like/as): 유령인 **것처럼** (as if it were a ghost). Compare: **-(으)ㄴ/는 듯** (as if — slightly more literary): 텅 빈 **듯** 보였다.

sentences.base_language: Donghyeon took one last look around and felt a mix of nostalgia and excitement about the future.
sentences.target_language: 동현은 마지막으로 한 번 주위를 둘러보면서, 향수와 미래에 대한 설렘이 뒤섞인 복잡한 감정을 느꼈다.
sentences.romanized: Donghyeon-eun majimak-euro han beon juwi-reul dulleobomyeonseo, hyangsu-wa mirae-e daehan seolleom-i dwisoekkin bokjaphan gamjeong-eul neukkkyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous action: 둘러보**면서** 느꼈다 (felt while looking around). **-면서** links two actions performed simultaneously by the same subject: 음악을 들으**면서** 공부하다 (study while listening to music). Here it captures Donghyeon taking his last look and experiencing the emotion at the very same moment — the looking triggers the feeling.
**-(으)ㄴ** past modifier on descriptive verbs/passive compounds: 뒤섞**인** 감정 (emotions that were mixed). **뒤섞이다** (to be mixed together — passive/intransitive) + **-(으)ㄴ**: the modifier describes the resulting state of the emotions — they have been blended. This is a common structure for describing complex inner states: 얽**힌** 감정 (tangled emotions), 뒤얽**힌** 관계 (entangled relationship).

sentences.base_language: The new apartment was on the third floor of a quiet building in a lively neighborhood.
sentences.target_language: 새 아파트는 활기찬 동네의 조용한 건물 3층에 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Sae apateu-neun hwalgichan dongne-eui joyonghan geonmul samcheung-e isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-에 있다** = to be located at/on: 3층**에 있었다** (was on the third floor). **에** marks a static location with verbs of existence such as 있다 and 없다: 학교**에 있다** (to be at school), 서울**에 있다** (to be in Seoul). This contrasts with **에서**, which marks the location of dynamic actions: 학교**에서** 공부하다 (study at school).
**활기차다** = to be lively, full of energy: **활기찬** 동네 (lively neighbourhood). **활기** (vitality/energy) + **차다** (to be full of): a compound adjective common in descriptions of places or people: **활기찬** 시장 (a bustling market), **활기차게** 움직이다 (move energetically). Compare: **북적이다** (to bustle — more about crowdedness), **활기차다** (to be energetic/lively — broader vitality).

sentences.base_language: When they arrived, a neighbor from the apartment next door came out to introduce herself and welcome Donghyeon.
sentences.target_language: 그들이 도착했을 때, 옆집에 사는 이웃이 나와서 자기소개를 하며 동현을 반갑게 맞이했다.
sentences.romanized: Geudeul-i dochaghaeosseul ttae, yeoptjib-e saneun iwut-i nawaseo jagi sogae-reul hamyeo Donghyeon-eul bangapge majihaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었을 때** = when [something had happened]: 도착했**을 때** (when they had arrived). The past-modifier form **-았/었을** in this pattern specifies a past point in time rather than a habitual or general time: 졸업했**을 때** (when [I] had graduated). Compare: 도착할 **때** (when [they] arrive — general/future), 도착했**을 때** (at the moment they had arrived — specific past event).
**-며** simultaneous: 자기소개를 하**며** 맞이했다 (welcomed while introducing herself). **-며** is the slightly more literary/concise form of **-면서**, connecting two concurrent actions: 웃**으며** 말했다 (spoke while smiling). Here it shows the introduction and the welcoming happening together as a single social gesture.

sentences.base_language: She told him there was a great bakery and a small traditional market just around the corner.
sentences.target_language: 그 이웃은 바로 모퉁이만 돌면 훌륭한 빵집과 작은 전통 시장이 있다고 알려 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Geu iwut-eun baro motungi-man dolmyeon hullyunghan ppangjib-gwa jageun jeontong sijang-i itdago allyeo jueotda.
literal: if you just turn the corner
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)면** conditional = if/when: 모퉁이를 돌**면** 있다 (if you turn the corner, it's there). **-(으)면** expresses a condition or natural consequence: 봄이 오**면** 꽃이 핀다 (if spring comes, flowers bloom). Combined with **바로** (just/right) and **만** (only), the phrase 바로 모퉁이**만** 돌**면** conveys 'you only have to turn the corner and it's right there' — a very natural way to give directions.
**알려 주다** = to inform/let someone know (as a favour): **알려 주었다** (told him, gave him the information). **알리다** (to inform — causative of 알다 'to know') + **주다** (benefactive): the compound emphasises that the information was shared as a helpful act. Compare: **말하다** (to say/speak — neutral), **알려 주다** (to let someone know — implies the recipient benefits from learning it).

sentences.base_language: Unloading the truck and carrying everything up the stairs took the rest of the afternoon.
sentences.target_language: 트럭에서 짐을 내리고 계단을 올라 모든 것을 나르는 작업이 오후 내내 이어졌다.
sentences.romanized: Teuleo-geseo jim-eul naerigo gyedan-eul olla modeun geot-eul nareuneun jageob-i ohu naenae ieojyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)는 것** nominalisation: 나르**는 것** → here used as a modifier on 작업, but the whole first clause is nominalised into a compound subject. Korean frequently builds noun phrases by stacking modifiers: 트럭에서 짐을 내리고 … 나르**는** 작업 (the task of unloading … and carrying). This long modifier-noun structure is characteristic of formal and written Korean.
**이어지다** = to continue, to be prolonged: 오후 내내 **이어졌다** (went on throughout the afternoon). **이어지다** (to be connected/continued — intransitive) derives from **잇다** (to connect). It describes an activity or state that stretches on without interruption: 회의가 **이어졌다** (the meeting dragged on), 작업이 **이어졌다** (the work continued). Compare: **계속되다** (to continue — more neutral/formal).

sentences.base_language: By the time the heavy furniture was in place, everyone was exhausted and drenched in sweat.
sentences.target_language: 무거운 가구를 다 제자리에 놓고 났을 때는, 모두 지쳐서 땀에 흠뻑 젖어 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Mugeooun gagu-reul da jejari-e nohgo nasseul ttae-neun, modu jichyeoseo ttam-e heumppeok jeo-jeo isseotda.
literal: by the time of having finished placing in position,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 있다** resultant state: 젖어 **있었다** (were drenched — in the state of having become soaked). **-아/어 있다** describes a state that results from a completed action and persists: 앉아 **있다** (is sitting — the state after having sat down), 열려 **있다** (is open — the state after having been opened). It differs from **-고 있다** which describes an ongoing action.
**-고 나다** completion + temporal clause: 놓**고 났을 때** (by the time of having finished placing). **-고 나다** signals full completion of the preceding action; adding **-았/었을 때** then creates a temporal clause pinpointing the moment after that completion. The combination 놓고 **났을 때** is more emphatic about finishing than simply 놓**았을 때** (when [they] had placed).

sentences.base_language: Donghyeon ordered several large pizzas to thank his friends for all their help.
sentences.target_language: 동현은 친구들의 도움에 감사하는 마음으로 큰 피자를 여러 판 시켰다.
sentences.romanized: Donghyeon-eun chingudeul-eui doum-e gamsahaneun maeum-euro keun pija-reul yeoreo pan sikyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)로** marking manner/state: 감사하는 마음**으로** 시켰다 (ordered with a grateful heart). **(으)로** attached to a noun expresses the manner, spirit, or state accompanying an action: 사랑**으로** 돌보다 (care for with love), 진심**으로** 말하다 (speak sincerely). This pattern personalises an action by specifying the emotional or motivational context behind it.
**시키다** = to order (food), to cause/make: 피자를 **시켰다** (ordered pizza). **시키다** is the causative of **하다** and also the standard colloquial verb for ordering food: 짜장면을 **시키다** (order jjajangmyeon), 배달을 **시키다** (order delivery). In this context it means placing a delivery order, more casual than **주문하다** (to place an order — formal).

sentences.base_language: They sat on the floor surrounded by boxes and ate eagerly, too tired to look for the bowls and chopsticks.
sentences.target_language: 모두 박스들에 둘러싸인 채 바닥에 앉아서, 그릇과 젓가락을 찾을 기력도 없이 열심히 먹었다.
sentences.romanized: Modu bakseudeul-e dulleossain chae badag-e anjaseo, geureut-gwa jeotgarak-eul chajeul giryeok-do eopsi yeolsimhi meogeotda.
literal: without even the energy to look for
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ 채** = while remaining in the state of, without changing from: 둘러싸인 **채** 앉아서 (sitting while surrounded [by boxes]). **-(으)ㄴ 채** attaches to a past modifier and indicates that an action occurs while a prior state is maintained unchanged: 신발을 신**은 채** 들어오다 (come in with shoes still on), 눈을 감**은 채** 기다리다 (wait with eyes closed). The key nuance is that the state persists unaltered.
**-도 없이** = without even: 기력**도 없이** (without even the energy). **도** (even) + **없이** (without): a combination that emphasises the lack of something to such an extent that even the baseline is absent: 말 한 마디**도 없이** (without even a single word), 연락**도 없이** (without even a message). It conveys exhaustion pushed to an extreme.

sentences.base_language: After his friends left, Donghyeon stood alone in the middle of the new space for the first time.
sentences.target_language: 친구들이 돌아가고 나서, 동현은 처음으로 혼자 새 공간 한가운데 서 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Chingudeul-i doragago naseo, Donghyeon-eun cheoeum-euro honja sae gongan hangaunde seo isseotda.
literal: after the friends had gone home,
in the middle of the new space
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 나서** = after doing (emphasising full completion): 돌아가**고 나서** (after [they had] gone home). **-고 나서** stresses that the first action is entirely finished before the second begins: 숙제를 끝내**고 나서** 놀았다 (played after finishing homework). Compare: **-(으)ㄴ 후에** (after — more neutral/formal): 돌아간 **후에** 혼자였다. **-고 나서** is more colloquial and emphasises the transition between the two states.
**서 있다** = to be standing (resultant state): **서 있었다** (was standing). **서다** (to stand up) + **-아/어 있다** (resultant state): the compound describes the continuing state of being upright after having stood. Compare: **서다** (the act of standing up — momentary), **서 있다** (the state of being in a standing position — ongoing). Other examples: 앉아 **있다** (to be seated), 누워 **있다** (to be lying down).

sentences.base_language: The rooms felt unfamiliar and hollow but somehow also full of possibility.
sentences.target_language: 방들은 낯설고 텅 빈 느낌이었지만, 왠지 모를 가능성으로도 가득 차 있는 것 같았다.
sentences.romanized: Bangdeul-eun natsseolgo teong bin neukkimi-eotjiman, wanji moreul ganeungseong-euro-do gadeuk cha inneun geot gatdda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-지만** concession: 느낌이었**지만** (it felt … but). **-지만** connects contrasting clauses: the rooms were hollow, yet also full of promise. It is more straightforwardly contrastive than **-는데** (which can be merely backgrounding): 힘들**지만** 행복하다 (hard but happy), 비싸**지만** 맛있다 (expensive but delicious).
**왠지 모를** = inexplicable, hard to say why: **왠지** (for some reason) + **모르다** (not know) + **-(으)ㄹ** modifier: literally 'that one cannot say why.' A very natural colloquial expression for an unnamed or vague quality: **왠지 모를** 불안감 (an unnameable anxiety), **왠지 모를** 설렘 (an inexplicable excitement). Compare the simpler **왠지** alone: 왠지 기분이 좋다 (for some reason I feel good).

sentences.base_language: He decided to unpack the kitchen first — that always made a new place feel more like home.
sentences.target_language: 동현은 부엌을 먼저 정리하기로 했다. 그렇게 하면 언제나 새 공간이 더 집처럼 느껴지곤 했으니까.
sentences.romanized: Donghyeon-eun bueok-eul meonjeo jeongnihagiро haetda. Geureoke hamyeon eonjena sae gongan-i deo jibcheoreom neukkkyeojigon haesseunikka.
literal: if you do it that way
used to come to feel (because, you see).
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기로 하다** = to decide to do: 정리하**기로 했다** (decided to organise). **-기로 하다** expresses a conscious decision or resolution: 일찍 자**기로 했다** (decided to go to bed early), 한국어를 배우**기로 했다** (decided to learn Korean). It is one of the most important decision-making patterns in Korean and pairs naturally with first-person subjects.
**-곤 하다** habitual past: 느껴지**곤 했으니까** (because it used to feel that way). **-곤 하다** attached to a past base describes something that happened repeatedly or habitually in the past: 어릴 때 여기 오**곤 했다** (used to come here as a child). The addition of **-(으)니까** at the end gives a reasoning/explanatory nuance — 'because, as you know, that's how it always worked.'

sentences.base_language: Finding the box marked 'bedding' turned into a long search and nearly caused a moment of panic.
sentences.target_language: '침구류'라고 적힌 박스를 찾는 것이 오랜 탐색으로 이어지더니 하마터면 패닉 상태가 될 뻔했다.
sentences.romanized: 'Chimguryu'-rago jeokhin bakseu-reul channeun geot-i oraen tamsaek-euro ieojideoeni hamateomyeon paenik sangtae-ga doel ppeonhaetda.
literal: the act of finding the box-SUBJECT
turned into a long search and then
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 뻔하다** = to almost do, to nearly happen: 될 **뻔했다** (nearly became). **-(으)ㄹ 뻔하다** describes an event that came very close to occurring but ultimately did not: 넘어질 **뻔했다** (almost fell), 놓칠 **뻔했다** (nearly missed it). It always implies the event did NOT happen — a narrow escape. Often paired with **하마터면** (almost/narrowly) for emphasis.
**-더니** observed sequence: 이어지**더니** … 될 뻔했다 (it turned into a long search and then nearly became panic). **-더니** here connects an observed development (the search dragging on) with its consequence (near-panic). The speaker/narrator perceived the first event unfolding before the second consequence emerged — a narrative sequencing tool that also carries a mild sense of 'and as a result.'

sentences.base_language: By midnight, the bed was assembled and the basics were in place.
sentences.target_language: 자정이 될 무렵에는 침대가 조립되어 있었고, 기본적인 것들이 제자리를 잡고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Jajeong-i doel mureop-eneun chimdae-ga joribdoeeo isseotgo, gibonjeok-in geotdeul-i jejari-reul jabgo isseotda.
literal: around the time it would become midnight,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 무렵** = around the time of, by the time: 자정이 될 **무렵** (around midnight / by the time midnight came). **무렵** is a noun meaning 'around that time' and takes the future modifier **-(으)ㄹ**: 해가 질 **무렵** (around sunset), 졸업할 **무렵** (around graduation time). It is more approximate than **-(으)ㄹ 때** and typically implies 'around about' that time.
**-아/어 있다** resultant state: 조립되어 **있었고** (was assembled — in the state of having been put together). **조립되다** (to be assembled — passive) + **-어 있다**: the bed had been assembled and remained in that completed state. Compare: **조립되었다** (was assembled — event), **조립되어 있었다** (was in an assembled state — ongoing result). The distinction parallels English 'was assembled' (event) vs 'was assembled' (state).

sentences.base_language: Donghyeon lay down exhausted, listening to the unfamiliar sounds of the new building and the street outside.
sentences.target_language: 동현은 지친 채로 누워서, 낯선 건물 소리와 바깥 거리의 소음에 귀를 기울였다.
sentences.romanized: Donghyeon-eun jichin chaero nuwoseo, natssen geonmul sori-wa bakat geori-eui soeum-e gwi-reul giuryeotda.
literal: in the state of being exhausted
to the noise of the street outside
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ 채로** = while remaining in the state of: 지친 **채로** 누웠다 (lay down while still exhausted). **채로** is a slightly more explicit form of **채** (see sentence 12), adding **로** (in the manner of) to reinforce that the state was maintained as-is: 옷을 입**은 채로** 잠들다 (fall asleep with clothes still on). It is interchangeable with **채** in most contexts but slightly more emphatic.
**귀를 기울이다** = to lend an ear, to listen attentively: **귀를 기울였다** (listened carefully). An idiomatic collocation — **귀** (ear) + **기울이다** (to tilt/incline): literally to tilt one's ear toward something. It implies active, focused listening rather than merely hearing: 이야기에 **귀를 기울이다** (listen closely to the story). Compare: **듣다** (to hear/listen — neutral), **귀를 기울이다** (to listen carefully — attentive engagement).

sentences.base_language: A distant clock struck midnight and Donghyeon finally felt something settle inside him — this was home now.
sentences.target_language: 멀리서 시계가 자정을 알리는 소리가 들리자, 동현은 마침내 마음속 깊은 곳에서 무언가가 자리를 잡는 것을 느꼈다. 이제 여기가 집이었다.
sentences.romanized: Meolliseo sigye-ga jajeong-eul allineun sori-ga deullija, Donghyeon-eun machimnae maeumsogeuk gipeun goseseo mueonkaga jari-reul janneun geot-eul neukkkyeotda. Ije yeogi-ga jib-ieotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** = as soon as, upon: 들리**자** (upon hearing / as soon as it was heard). **-자** marks immediate succession — the feeling arose the very instant the clock was heard. More literary and instantaneous than **-면** (if/when) or **-을 때** (when): 문이 열리**자** 빛이 쏟아졌다 (as soon as the door opened, light poured in). It lends a cinematic precision to narrative moments.
**자리를 잡다** = to settle, to find one's place: 무언가가 자리를 **잡는** 것을 느꼈다 (felt something settle). Beyond its literal sense of 'taking a seat' (see sentence 4 of the reference story), **자리를 잡다** metaphorically means to become established or to find equilibrium: 마음이 자리를 **잡다** (the heart settles), 새 습관이 자리를 **잡다** (a new habit takes hold). The nominalised form **잡는 것을 느끼다** (feel the act of settling) creates a vivid inner-state description.

sentences.base_language: As he drifted off to sleep, he felt grateful for good friends and quietly excited about everything the new neighborhood would bring.
sentences.target_language: 잠이 들어 가면서, 동현은 좋은 친구들에게 감사함을 느꼈고, 새 동네가 가져다 줄 모든 것에 대해 조용히 설레고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Jam-i deureo gamyeonseo, Donghyeon-eun joeun chingudeul-ege gamsaham-eul neukkkyeotgo, sae dongne-ga gajeoda jul modeun geot-e daehae joyonghi seollego isseotda.
literal: as sleep was coming on (drifting off)-while,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 가다** directional/progressive: 잠이 들어 **가면서** (while drifting off to sleep). **-아/어 가다** marks movement away from the current state or a process gradually advancing: 알아 **가다** (gradually come to know), 나아 **가다** (gradually improve/move forward). 잠이 들어 **가다** captures sleep gradually overtaking Donghyeon — a process in motion — rather than a sudden onset.
**가져다 주다** future modifier: 가져다 **줄** 모든 것 (everything [the neighbourhood] will bring). The future modifier **-(으)ㄹ** turns 가져다 주다 into an attributive: 가져다 **줄** 것들 (things that will be brought/given). This pattern is very common for describing anticipated future benefits: 일어날 **일** (the things that will happen), 바뀔 **것들** (the things that will change). It closes the story with forward-looking optimism.
